canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, Task generalization is most commonly described as a model's capacity to perform effectively on new or unseen tasks that were not included in its training or fine-tuning data. This ability is frequently characterized by the transfer of learned capabilities to different tasks, domains, or data distributions, often without requiring task-specific retraining, a condition specified in definitions of 'Cross-task generalization' and 'Generality'. While the core idea of performing on unseen tasks is consistent, usage varies: 'Multi-task generalization' often refers to maintaining performance across a diverse set of tasks within a unified architecture, whereas 'Task-level generalization' is used in the context of robotic policies adapting to novel simulation scenarios. A less common framing, 'Single-task generalization', diverges from this by focusing on a model's performance on a single, specific task after parameter-efficient fine-tuning. To operationalize this construct, researchers evaluate models on broad, multi-task benchmarks. For instance, the MMLU benchmark, which covers 57 distinct tasks, is explicitly used to measure a model's 'general task-solving abilities' and performance on 'previously unseen tasks'. The construct is also studied in relation to other phenomena, with some work positing that it is influenced by factors such as 'Parameter interference' and 'Trajectory retrieval'.

definitions.text: The capacity of the model to transfer learned capabilities across a wide variety of distinct image-processing tasks without task-specific fine-tuning.
definitions.paper: PromptFix: You Prompt and We Fix the Photo

definitions.text: The ability of a model trained on a specific source task, such as node classification, to perform well on a different, unseen target task, such as link prediction, without additional fine-tuning.
definitions.paper: LLMs as Zero-shot Graph Learners: Alignment of GNN Representations with LLM Token Embeddings

definitions.text: The ability of an embodied agent to adapt learned behavior to new tasks across domains and prompts beyond the training distribution.
definitions.paper: GenRL: Multimodal-foundation world models for generalization in embodied agents

definitions.text: The model's preserved competence across diverse domains and tasks beyond the specific one it has been fine-tuned on, reflecting robustness and transferability.
definitions.paper: Large Language and Protein Assistant for Protein-Protein Interactions Prediction

definitions.text: The ability of a model to perform well on a single, specific downstream task after parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
definitions.paper: Come Together, But Not Right Now: A Progressive Strategy to Boost Low-Rank Adaptation

definitions.text: The ability of a method or model to perform effectively across a variety of different datasets and task types without task-specific training.

The latent ability of a robotic policy to perform well on unseen tasks after training on a diverse set of simulation tasks, reflecting broad conceptual understanding rather than overfitting to specific instances.
definitions.paper: GenSim: Generating Robotic Simulation Tasks via Large Language Models

definitions.text: The model's ability to perform effectively on tasks that were not part of its training set, based only on provided instructions.
definitions.paper: Instructive Decoding: Instruction-Tuned Large Language Models are Self-Refiner from Noisy Instructions

definitions.text: The model's ability to perform well on tasks and task categories that were not included in its fine-tuning data.
definitions.paper: Pushing Mixture of Experts to the Limit: Extremely Parameter Efficient MoE for Instruction Tuning

definitions.text: The ability to perform well on function classes not seen during pretraining, which arises when the pretraining task distribution is sufficiently diverse and represents a deviation from strict Bayesian prediction.
definitions.paper: In-Context Learning through the Bayesian Prism

definitions.text: The ability of a model to leverage information learned from a set of source tasks to improve learning and performance on new, unseen target tasks.
definitions.paper: LiFT: Learning to Fine-Tune via Bayesian Parameter Efficient Meta Fine-Tuning

relationships.paper: Rethinking Channel Dimensions to Isolate Outliers for Low-bit Weight Quantization of Large Language Models
relationships.evidence: We further evaluate the LLMs before and after M2MS instruction tuning on the MMLU evaluation dataset (Hendrycks et al., 2021). The MMLU dataset covers 57 tasks including elementary mathematics, US history, computer science, law, etc, and is designed to evaluate models’ world knowledge and problem-solving ability. (Section 6)
